I purchased this projection screen to improve the image quality for my portable projector over the bare wall that I was using currently.  Unfortunately, I had to return it because it did not do what I needed.  This projection screen is made of a translucent, white fabric that is quite stretchy, which are both bad properties to have for a front-projection screen.  Most people will be mounting their projection screen against a wall so being a translucent fabric, much of the projected light passes through the screen and hits the wall, then reflect back on to the screen.  The net effect of this is that there is a fuzzy glow around everything in the image.  Furthermore, being a stretchy fabric, you will end up with ugly curved edges in an effort to mount the screen tightly in order to remove any creases.  What you end up with on your wall is something that looks like you skinned a polar bear and a projected image that is akin to looking through a thick layer of fog.  I would not recommend this projection screen for anything other than a temporary screen that you might put up because there isn't a flat wall near by (camping?)

[[VIDEOID:8bed6d79d90a43dd042702aefe52b20a]] I wanted to love this phone so badly. Now that LG has left the phone market, I wanted to know there are still flagship killers like Oneplus out there to take on the bullies of wireless phones. And on paper, there's a LOT to love about this phone. Let's mention the good things first: - As a photographer, I really like the Hasselblad cameras that include a very capable manual mode. See the included photos taken with the phone, no fake bokeh effects. That's just the natural blurring DOF of the lens and sensor. - As someone who is picky about user experience I absolutely LOVE the silent/vibrate/ring physical switch. All smartphones need that, in my opinion. - CPU/GPU benchmarks are awesome, right up there with flagships that cost more. - Coming from the Galaxy S10's terrible in-screen fingerprint reader, this one renews my faith in fp readers. - Display is beautiful and crisp. - Battery is surprisingly good.  In a nutshell, the hardware is great! If they just throw a stock Android OS on here, it would be perfect! But sadly, no... they didn't. And the software is TERRIBLE. Chock full of bugs. Some examples (some of which I tried to capture on video): 1) Google Voice call redirects are broken. I've been using GV as my phone number for nearly a decade and never had a single problem like this before. The stock dialer does not care what you set GV to, it will IGNORE your outgoing call redirect requests. No matter what you set it to, it uses the carrier number to call out. Even if you select "ask before each call", the phone is so excited to dial out it will show you the option to pick, but in the background IT STARTED CALLING ALREADY and clearly doesn't care what you want. To be fair, this started with the Android 12 update on the Oneplus 9 series as well, so it's a Oneplus problem in general, it seems.  2) Android Auto pop ups make no sense. When you plug the phone into a USB port, it will prompt you with three options. Charge, Transfer Files / Android Auto, and Transfer Photos. When I plugged it into my car with AA, I see it starting on my car's display, but the pop up still appears on the phone. Naturally, I picked option 2, since it says android auto. It disconnects. So I unplug and try again, and leave it on charge, which seems to work. For the sake of testing, I also tried transfer photos, and it also worked. It seems that THE ONLY OPTION THAT DOESN'T WORK is the one that says Android Auto on it! Seriously?! Is it opposite day?! But that's ok, my car radio also supports wireless Android Auto (uses Wifi direct instead of a cable), so that should avoid the problem right?  3) Wireless Android Auto breaks Wifi's ability to reconnect to known networks. I know what you're thinking... come on, right? How many Google features can Oneplus break at a time. Apparently a lot. When using Android Auto over the wireless connection, it actually works great, but then the phone seems to give up ever wanting to connect to my known wifi networks again. I get to work or return home and found out that I've been using cellular data the entire time, unless I manually go into Wifi settings and click join network. If I leave the coverage area or toggle wifi off/on, it won't reconnect. I have to manually connect it in settings each time or be stuck using my data plan. A reboot fixes the problem and the wifi reconnects as expected afterwards... until the next time I take the car somewhere and the problem comes back.  4) The 3.3x Telephoto camera has its own color science. It doesn't match the others. The main camera is actually quite good, and the wide is decent as well. The telephoto is a bit less vibrant and noticeably more dull on warm color themes. I though these were Hasselblad tuned and calibrated to have professional color science and detail? This isn't a major issue, it just seems surprising. Especially when you point the camera at something that is too dark for the limited exposure capability of the 3.3x tele lens, and it jumps to a zoomed in version of the main camera lens instead. Seriously- see my video for a demonstration of that. You see the color suddenly shift into something that looks very different whenever it changes. The main zoomed in actually looks better, and it has enough native pixels to resolve a decent image... which then begs the question of why even have a dedicated 3.3x tele lens?  4) The notifications in the shade don't let you jump to the application... only individual messages. For example, if I have a bunch of Gmail messages, I can see them in the shade and click them individually to go to them each. But I can't just go to Gmail and see my inbox. Sometimes I want to see my inbox and decide what to respond to first. I have to either go to an individual message, or back out and find the Gmail icon to click it directly.  All in all, the phone is usable but I just can't shake the feeling that the OS is half baked and not ready for release yet. The Android Auto issues and Google Voice ones are so major to the way I use my phone on a daily basis, I might prefer to go back to a device from 3 years ago than continue using this one. It just feels unpredictable and unreliable in its current state. I really do hope Oneplus fixes those bugs soon, because the hardware really is nice. Or better yet, give us the option to run a stock Android without all this OOS nonsense. The customizations aren't a selling point, they are detrimental at best and downright BREAK CERTAIN FUNCTIONALITY at worst. I will gladly change my review and update the rating if an update that addresses these issues comes around. Come on, Oneplus. We know you can do better than this on a phone that costs this much.

For performance, I would rate this a solid 5-stars.  I bought a quality $300 router before this one and it just wouldn't fully cover the house with adequate wi-fi strength.  I spent $100 more on an extender, which finally helped somewhat, but still not ideal.  The router in this D-Link system has good strong wi-fi signal by itself, but it includes an extender which make it even better.  Excellent wi-fi coverage and also works good for wired connections as well.  For the price, the D-Link router is well worth the money.  So why not 5 stars in my rating?  The setup instructions turned out to be useless for me, but may have been because my internet was coming in through a router/modem combo; multiple routers on a network can cause issues and change how you might access a given router to set it up.  Support is important, especially when you are trying to set up for the first time, but I tried to call and there was a 25 minute wait, so decided to e-mail instead.  That was several days ago and the e-mail has not been answered yet, so I am disappointed in support, so that's one reason I knocked one star off my rating.  I strongly recommend you download the manual from their website, as the unit doesn't come with it.  The manual seems to be well written and comprehensive.  It still didn't help me with setup; I had to figure that out on my own.  If you have a straight-forward setup that is not complicated, you may not have any issues with setting up this unit.  Since this and most routers I have seen only have connections for 4 wired items, I am running an "extension" switch for more network devices and computer.  I could not get it to work at first, until a network expert told me that you have to reboot the router after you connect the switch, in order for it to be recognized.

Let me preface this review by explaining that I work in the IT industry and support a variety of businesses with enough thorough experience in networking to say with certainty - the router I received is absolute garbage.  Now, normally I'd say for this price that you should set you expectations quite low - being in the field I'm in, I understand that almost all consumer routers are not great options, and the ones that are somewhat viable are on the $300+ range. Not everybody has the money or cares enough to invest this much - I get that.  So, that leaves me and my old roommate pretty much one option; the TRENDnet Wireless AC1750. He doesn't need much and especially doesn't want to spend much, it was tough getting him to go high enough in price to even this router- the old router he had came with his cable subscription 3 years ago. It worked fine but was sometimes slow with downloading over WiFi, not surprising. From the research and reviews, and most importantly the price - we decided to try this Trendnet AC1750 router.  WOW what garbage - he no longer gets WiFi in his bathroom corner of the house - it is a VERY small 2 bedroom apartment. How in the world can this router not handle a signal from 20 feet away, with only 2 walls in between? A router of this caliber (even at this price) should EASILY be able to handle covering a 2 bedroom small apartment. Period.  On to the second problem - even if we're connected, the WiFi signal drops constantly, I have to now reset this router every single day by unplugging it and plugging it back in because it refuses to work longer than 16 hours before crapping out. I have tried updating firmware, all network resets I can. I've even tried using the old modem (we replaced both router + modem) to see if that was the root of our problems - nope, it's the router.  It has slowly been getting worse and worse, and when I am gone from this apartment my roommate will be forced to reset his router every day, sometimes twice a day, just to get internet - or purchase a new one.  I am extremely dissatisfied with this product, although not quite surprised or disappointed given its price. It just seems odd that a 3 year old Netgear N300 router performs much better than an AC1750 Dual Band router.

I have a couple of Raspberry Pi zeros that use the mini HDMI sized plugs. I needed an adapter, so I searched Amazon for a 2 pack, and these even say compatible with Raspberry Pi in the title / description. I can assure you THEY ARE NOT COMPATIBLE WITH THE RASPBERRY PI. As far as I know, the Pi Zero is the only board that uses this sort of HDMI plug (the others use full sized HDMI), and the angle of this adapter makes it IMPOSSIBLE to connect the DC power input at the same time.  You can clearly see in the attached image, the HDMI cable blocks access to the input. If it was angled the other direction, it would work fine but in its current design, it DOES NOT work with any Pi board that I'm aware of. It does work as an adapter for different hardware - my laptop for example has mini HDMI and my cameras use micro. So maybe I'll keep them around for that, but I'm only giving it 2 stars because I bought it based on the claim it would work for a specific product mentioned in the description, and it most certainly does not.

An amazing product with excellent picture quality and valuable functionality.  Pros: Picture quality is better than my Samsung TV (comparable model) only 6 months older. Specifically with HDR content. Color is more vibrant. Ability to view and any angle is very good. Still looks bright when viewing from an angel. Easily able to mount on the wall with universal mounting kit (by myself) in less than an hour. (Note: It comes with some extenders that you need to screw in or to work with mounting bracket, this took me 20 min's to figure out) You can access some of the inputs from the side, but many you will need to plug in before you mount it. No lag with PlayStation or Xbox play. Processor does a great job with high speed motion processing. Comprehensive list of apps. Works will with Netflix, Prime, Google Play and Plex. Voice commands are helpful. Easily able to tell it what to do, where to go.  Cons: My only con is the busy remote. Samsung really outshines Sony here. It's a simple design that let's me navigate without even looking at the remote. With the classic Sony remote, I always have to look at it to navigate, too big and too many buttons. Also the center dial (navigation wheel) is too small, it's used most and should be more prominent so my large thumbs can easily click it.  Conclusion A flagship TV from Sony that offers excellent image quality from a quality brand.

Photoboxes are simple things; It is usually just 5-sided box with some sort of backdrop and a strip of LED.  The Heysliy Light Box Photography lightbox is just that and nothing more.  Designed to be portable, the box can be folded flat and comes with a carrying bag that nicely stores everything in the kit.  Unfortunately, it's the execution of the thing that is to be desired.  The box is cut from a sheet of translucent plastic then folded to form a box.  The flaps are joined by a barely-tight-enough screw cap that can be easily lost because it is not actually part of the box.  The plastic itself is stuff, but has an elastic memory, which means that it wants to fold back to its storage position, weakening the sides of the box unless the flaps are folded outward first.  Overall, the structure is quite flimsy and can be tricky to set up.  The Heysliy lightbox comes with four backdrops made of that paper-like material that is used by some reuseable grocery bags.  The colors are black, white, green, and red.  The backdrop is hung by a hole cut into the material and a tab that's cut into the plastic box.  Any hard tug will rip that backdrop so be careful.  So the conclusion is that the box is annoying to set up and with the LED's mounted on the inside of the box, reflections will be a big issue with this box as can be seen in my pictures (nintendo switch pictured for size reference).

This is advertised as an ergonomic keyboard.  To me, ergonomic is a keyboard that is split into two slanted halves, like the Microsoft Natural Keyboard.  This is a flat keyboard.  In spite of that, the keys have a good feel and response.  I only have wired keyboards, so I got this so that I could have a wireless if I wanted to use one sometimes, as you are then not confined with a cord.  Requires 1 AA battery to operate (not included).  At first, I thought they forgot to include the wireless transmitter, but it comes hidden in the battery compartment.  This is okay, but there is no place to store it once the battery is installed.  Of course, if you are going to store the keyboard and not use it all the time, you wouldn't typically want to leave the battery in anyway.  If you want wireless, probably better to get a wireless mouse and keyboard together, as they would probably run off one transmitter, whereas with this keyboard if I want to add a wireless mouse later, it will involve using a second transmitter which would use another USB port.  The keyboard is going to be of limited use to me, as I currently use a KVM-switch that allows me to control up to 4 computers with one monitor, one mouse and one keyboard.  I would have to upgrade to a KVM switch that takes USB to use the keyboard on more than one computer.  This is truly plug and play and I had it operating in no time at all.  Seems like it is quality made and should last a while.  Made in China of course.

My previous model was an older model Viewsonic (don't recall the exact model, but it was an 800x600 one), and I had a very positive experience with it. I use this both for home entertainment and the occasional work-related presentation, so when my old one broke (fell off a table), I decided to go with another Viewsonic.  But I've got to admit, something seems to have went wrong with this model. I love using HDMI to my laptop to project vs VGA, however the first day I did this, when my laptop went to "sleep", the projector started going crazy. Waking up the laptop put it into some sort of loop, it would try to power on, the laptop would report there is no external monitor, the projector would shut off, the laptop would detect something is there just as it was powering down, projector would try to power up again, repeat. I unplugged the HDMI, the laptop went back to normal, but the projector would continue doing this until I pulled the power.  After that, I just made sure not to let the HDMI sleep when using the projector, and its been working OK. Wrote this off as a silly quirk, the picture quality is better than my old one so I'd just live with it. Until the day I was using it for a presentation at a fundraising dinner full of guests. The thing shut off every 10-15 minutes, apparently overheating. The little red light would come on and refuse to let me power it back on for a couple of minutes at a time. I had used my old Viewsonic in exactly the same situation and had NEVER had a problem like this before! After leaving it unplugged for a while, I was able to get it to come on long enough to make it through the presentation, but a couple of days later it did the same thing at another location, and I was forced to rent a backup projector.  Mind you, I'm in my third month of using the projector, I've clocked maybe 10-15 hours total on it. I brought it home, hooked it up to my entertainment center, and I was unable to recreate the problem. Worked just fine a couple of days ago. I just tried it again now, and it won't power up- keeps spinning the fan, then letting out a beep, stopping, and trying again. Like its stuck in that loop again, but there is nothing even attached to it yet! No lamp lighting up, no idea what's going on. If I leave it unplugged, it will probably start working again soon, but this has been totally unreliable for me! I'm hoping I got a lemon and perhaps Viewsonic will do something for me (Its too late to return to Amazon). I will update this review if so. Otherwise, I'm extremely disappointed in this model!

Have used this keyboard for many years at work and home and I wouldn't use any other keyboard.  It is that comfortable.  I would hate to pay the price they are asking for these now.  Hate to say this for those of you who want one, but I just bought one for $2.99 from Goodwill.  It is well worth it to have a spare on hand; actually have 2 spares, as obviously these are expensive to replace if you buy new. These keyboards have both USB and PS/2 type connectors and two USB ports in the back of the keyboard which I have never used.  I tried out the USB ports in the back and they do not work.  There could be a couple of reasons for this.  One might be that in order for them to function, you might have to use the USB connector for the keyboard, rather than the round PS/2 connector.  Another reason is that my keyboard does not connect directly to the computer, but goes through a KVM switch.  Through the switch, the keyboard may not receive enough power to power the USB ports.  I don't have a USB connector on my KVM switch, so I have to use the PS/2 round connector.  The only problem that I have encountered with this keyboard is the feet that elevate the back of the keyboard to a comfortable angle, tend to fall off.  I have lost a couple and cannot find replacements.  If you are used to a flat keyboard, the tilted ergo keyboard does take some getting used to, but once you have used it for a while, you will never go back to a flat keyboard again.  Microsoft has some newer versions of this keyboard, but I don't think they are an improvement and they are expensive.

I have used this product for 5 years, and it works fine.  Just make sure you format for NTFS, else you may experience a nightmare below.  Do not read below, unless you feel necessary NOT TO FORMAT ( and be stupid, like me. )  ------------  I am editing the stars to 4 star - but cannot do it on amazon.  After you understand the pitfalls (you must format this drive NTFS first,) then all other aspects of it are very good.  So, below -- just keep in mind that I was "upset".  If you do not format this external drive - be ready for some real problems.  Forget the 2-page manual that says "preformatted."  You need to format this one.  Or else ...  The WD 250 boasts on being an external drive, but neglects to mention that it must be unplugged from the computer before power down.  Well, clumbsy me, I made the same mistake twice with this product.  The desktop and the laptop both recognized the MyBook drive "ok" and had no problem writing data files to it.  So, I thought all is well.  But, in both cases, once the PC was turned off - it became a nightmare.  Since both my PCs run in XP Prof mode, with all service pack software updated, latest and greatest versions of everything, I would assume nothing should go wrong.  Not so.  In both cases, the Xp operating system was damaged to "the point of no return."  Furthermore, all data on my PCs were lost. Now - why did I buy this external hard drive?  Ok,  this is not the first time some hardware has gone wrong on my PCs, but usually it does not take out the entire operating system - such that I cannot run a "restore," and get back to working.  After calling WD on the phone, they suggested that I must unplug the MyBook each and everytime, before turning off the PCs.  I would have given this external drive a 4 star rating - if it was not for the bad experiences, and the loss of time, loss of data from my PC.

This is a VERY cool device.  Packaged with the antenna variety pack provides out-the-box reception across a broad range of frequencies supported by this software defined receiver.  Following the quick start instructions on the rtl-sdr blog web site, I was up and running with a Windows 10 laptop in under 10 minutes.  I played with the thing for an hour straight!  So many neat features, and I've only scratched the surface with lots more to learn.  Great for visualization and spotting frequencies with signals, much faster than scanning for frequencies on my handy-talkie.  Great for beginners (no HAM license required since it's receive only, no transmit) and great for licensed HAMs looking to broaden their view across a wider world of RF.

This was much heavier and larger than I expected.  It would be nice if it had a carrying handle on top, but since it runs on house current and not batteries, it's not portable, so the handle would only be useful for moving it.  The weight is probably because of the high quality built-in speakers.  The sound quality is excellent, compared to even good quality computer speakers.  It also sounds better than my stereo speakers.  The manual leaves a little to be desired, but the system is pretty easy to operate and most operations are intuitive.  The biggest disappointment I had is that the AM radio does not work; it is apparently set up for European standards and won't work in the U.S. from what I was able to find out. It is disclosed in the description that AM is not available in the U.S.  It does get FM stations with good fidelity and has an FM aerial.  There is an external input on the back, so you can play through the unit, which you might want to do because of the good sound quality.  They also include a connecting cable for this, which is a nice bonus.  Unfortunately no external output, which could be handy, but not a necessity.  Just insert a CD and it starts playing the CD right away.  Insert a USB stick and it starts playing any audio files on the stick.  At first I thought there was no slot for a USB stick, as it is not identified that way in the manual; it is identified as a phone charging port, a U disk port (maybe they meant USB) and for I-Pod reading.  This is just a one-CD at a time system; would be great if it could take multiple CDs.  The remote is okay and has all the functions you'll need, but is a little confusing at first.  I didn't test the Bluetooth connection capability to a cell phone, as I don't think I would use that function.  If you want a really nice sounding CD player than also handles other audio files on a USB or SD card that also has FM radio and you don't care about the AM radio not working, this is worth getting.  Would give this 5 stars if the AM radio worked.  This is actually a VW automobile disk player that has been converted by putting a wooden case around it and adding quality speakers; very clever idea, especially since this is a pretty decent CD player.  Also they include a demo CD with 18 tracks of popular well known songs, which is another bonus.

I paired and typed with this keyboard on the following four devices:  1. iPad3 2. iPhone5 3. BLU Life One 4G LTE Smartphone - GSM Unlocked 4. Nexus 7 3rd generation  It paired easily with the first two iOS (Apple) devices.  However, the first-time pairing with the Android devices (items 3 and 4) took a lot of trial and error. I spent about 20 minutes trying to get each Android device to recognize the keyboard. I wound up charging the keyboard thinking it lacked power but it had a sufficient charge. When charging the keyboard did not help the situation, I turned the keyboard on and off, turned Bluetooth on and off, kept trying the pairing button with each attempt, etc., etc. Eventually, it finally worked but not without a lot of frustration. Also, with one of my devices, it paired using the MAC address of the keyboard and not the name.  In order to pair a Bluetooth device, one has to: - Turn on the keyboard. Note that keyboard light will flash blue but only for a few seconds and it’s hard to see it. (Sometimes it did not flash for me, instead it was a steady blue). - Enable Bluetooth on the device you are pairing it with. The device must be no more than 32 feet from keyboard. - Press and hold “pair device” button on keyboard for about 5 seconds--LED light starts flashing on keyboard. - When the “Bluetooth Wireless Keyboard” is found on your device, select it to complete the pairing process. - To maximize keyboard functionality, once paired press FN + S for an iOS device and FN + A for an Android device. It’s at this point that the keyboard is ready to use.  There are multi-function hot keys like Shift + ~ to return to the Home Page or Shift + ! to begin a Search using the default browser on the paired device.  However, the best use of the keyboard is for straight typing, in my opinion.  PROS  - I like the feel of the keyboard. It’s pliable, folds easily, and does not slip out of my hands.  - A micro-USB charging cable is supplied for charging the keyboard.  - Supposedly, this is waterproof. However, I did not dare submerge it in water. I will take their word for it.  CONS  - This keyboard is not designed for a touch typist like me. For instance, the QWERTY keyboard layout is a bit off because the B key is located on the right-hand side of the fold. I use my left hand to hit the B key but with this keyboard, I either have to over stretch my index finger to reach the B key or use my right hand.  - There are no risers for the keyboard; instead it lies very flat. In order for me to effectively type on this, I have to prop the top end on a book so that the keyboard lays on a slant.  - The keys are not sensitive to the touch, so I have to press hard in order for the letters to type.  - It’s difficult to determine when the keyboard is powered on. Many times I had to press and hold the power button until the light flashed as a simple press of the button would not work. The instructions say the blue light flashes for three seconds when on but this did not always happen for me. Sometimes the blue light came on steady indicating the keyboard was on.  It was not always consistent.  I wound up either pressing the pairing button to see if the pairing light would flash or just tried typing when paired with a device. Depending on whether the keyboard worked or not, I knew whether it was on or not.  On another note, when I registered this keyboard on the VisionTek site, I got a confirmation email with my username and password in clear text. This is poor security as passwords should not be sent in clear text over the Internet.  In summary, had I tried out this keyboard in a store, I would have never selected it. Unless I am typing a lot with my Nexus 7, this is just too difficult to use due to the effort that has to be used to type, the flatness of the keyboard, and the slightly odd layout. I love the feel of the keyboard and its compactness but other than this, I’m disappointed.

I like the basic design of this -- the small sleek size, nice layout of buttons, large display.  The radio reception seems to be decent, although mounting it on the wall is not possible in my area, since I have to turn it various directions to get a couple of local AM stations.  Weather band reception is OK, as is FM reception.  That's about where my positive feelings for this little radio end.  It is way too difficult to set up, NOT with regard to the physical aspects of hitting the correct buttons in the correct sequence, but rather in terms of deciphering and understanding the instructions.  The instruction leaflet, such as it is, is written in such small type that it's nearly impossible to read.  I finally went to the Eton website and downloaded the instruction manual there, then "zoom"ed it so I could read how to set the time, store radio stations, etc.  Then there's the issue of the S.A.M.E. codes.  I had never heard of this technology before I got this radio.  NO WHERE in the included instructions is there any mention of NOAA, or even the "S.A.M.E." designation. On the Amazon product page, there is a mention of "S.A.M.E." in one of the product features bullets, and "NOAA" in another product feature bullet on the product page here on Amazon, but nothing that explains what "S.A.M.E. codes" actually ARE.  The instruction manual tells you how to "Select the county" -- OK, so what does THAT mean?  The instructions don't tell you that "Select the county" really means "enter the NOAA S.A.M.E. codes."  You're not "selecting" anything; you need to know the S.A.M.E. code(s) for the desired area(s), and then ENTER those according to the instructions.  This was my first weather radio -- and I'm generally fairly good at figuring things out -- but this had me confused for a good 15-20 minutes while I searched via google for "county code," "weather county code," etc.  Finally found the NOAA site and the codes, and entered the one for my county.  And I STILL don't know what "Setting Defeat" means.  I mean, I know how to set it, and I can read what the manual says, but it doesn't really tell me WHY I might want to enable or disable "defeat," and what, precisely, it enables or disables ("ENABLE: Alert when receiving a message. DISABLE: Displays the message on the LCD when receiving a message" -- does that mean that when it's enabled, you DON'T get a message displayed on the LCD when receiving a message? What kind of Alert happens when it's enabled? the flashing light? the siren? what, precisely, am I "defeating" with this??)  I have no idea if the weather alert part of the radio works.  We've had one National Weather Service-issued high wind warning here in the Pacific NW since I received and programmed the radio, and it didn't set off the alert -- no light flashing, no "message," nothing.  I intend to take this along on an extended road trip this summer (reprogramming the S.A.M.E. codes along the route), and I figure I'll probably encounter some sort of "alert-able" weather event along the way.  I'll update this review then if that part works.  Meanwhile, I'm giving this 2 stars -- it works pretty well as a compact "clock radio," doesn't take up a lot of space, and has decent reception.  But Eton REALLY needs to do something about the instructions.  The ones included with the product are useless, and the copy on the website isn't much better.

This card is exactly what I wanted, small with low power requirements.  I used it in a home theater computer and it played every HD movie I tested smoothly and cleanly.  The problem is there is something funny with it's temperatures.  At first it was fine, running 42 to 53 degrees but then on the 3rd day I turned it off to install a network card and burned myself on the video card's heat sink.  When I had the computer back up and running I checked the video card's temperature and it was idling at 103 degrees, Celsius.  I let it run for a few more minutes while I installed Evga's precision software to verify that temp and it agreed with hwmonitor.  I shut it down.  A couple hours later I brought it up again, this time it was idling at 63 degrees and didn't vary much from that temp for the next several hours.  The next day it was up in the eighties and then again nineties. Today it stayed around 71.  I really don't have a good idea what is wrong with it and I'm not going to spend anymore time worrying about it for a brand new card.  It's going back.

I thought I wouldn’t be picky when it comes to buying a new alarm clock; I mean, I owned a Sony Dream Machine (yeah, you too?) for over 30 years and that had minimal features!  At least, that’s what I thought until I actually started looking for a new one.  When you look at the possibilities out there you start to think that perhaps I need:  - Bluetooth?  It would be nice to wake up to a playlist I set up in my phone.  - Good sound quality?  If I’m gonna have bluetooth, the sound quality needs to be good.  - Projection?  It would be nice to see the time on the ceiling with the press of a button.  - Atomic? It would be nice to NOT have to manually set the time for Daylight Savings.  - Temperature/Weather?  It would be great to know the weather when I look at the time -- especially if it is projected on the ceiling!  The Soundbot tries to provide many of these features but fail to put them together into one cohesive unit.  It tries to be an alarm clock and bluetooth speakers but fails at both.  The Good:  - Great looking design.  The wood look and the size, color and placement of time and temperature all add to a great look for the Soundbot.  - Impressive sound.  As I’m currently searching for a bluetooth portable speaker (and have already reviewed a few), I pay special attention to sound quality.  It’s comparable to the Jambox Mini.  - Touch controls.  No buttons to detract from the wood look of the clock.  The snooze, volume, and phone buttons are all touch sensitive.  - Time is remembered even when unplugged.  The Bad:  - The clock does NOT play a bluetooth when the alarm goes off.  The workaround is to use your phone as the alarm, but what’s the point?  - No Next/Prev track buttons.  The +/- buttons only control volume.  - Only one level of dimming.  You can choose full or half brightness with the snooze button but it is still a little bright for me.  I have to turn the clock away while I sleep.  - Alarm only has one sound: an annoying beep.  Tip: The alarm clock can be set to go off every day (the alarm LED is solid, not flashing).  When the alarm goes off, PRESS and HOLD any touch-sensitive button to disable the alarm for today.  It will then go off again tomorrow.  Conclusion: With the inability to use the bluetooth as an alarm tone, and without an FM Radio as an alternative, the Soundbot serves as a pretty poor alarm clock.  You’re stuck with a simple beep.  Without a next/prev track button, the Soundbot serves as a pretty poor wireless speaker.  The good sound quality is the only thing it has going for it.

So these are not bad cameras or great ones.  They do seem to either miss important events or overcapture little ones like bugs flying past.  My biggest issue is that I now own two outdoor cameras that would work if they could be charged.  I use them with solar panels but after a few months I get a message that they need to be charged.  I thought maybe this was because we had some not sunny days but now I am realizing that the port that the usb cord plugs into gets broken.  I don't know if it is from wind or just cheap products but when I took each camera in to charge with the supplied cord from the box, the port sunk into the camera.  There is no way to get them out.  I tried to unscrew the camera to get it from the inside but the plastic seems to be welded on as even unscrewed I cannot open the camera.  I contacted the company when it happened the first time and they said there was nothing they could do about it as the warranty expired.  Then I asked them  how to open it up so I could try to push it out and they said just undo the screws.  When I told them that didn't work, they did not respond.  For the second camera - it is a replacement they sent me from yet a different issue on the original.  I have only had it up for 6 months and got the same message about needing it to charge.  I brought it in from the solar panel and as I plugged the cord into it, the port pushed all the way in again.  No way to access it.  REally bad design flaw.  I am waiting to hear back from the company but expect them to tell me it is out of warranty - even though it was a replacement given to me only 6 months ago, prior to the original camera's warranty expiration.  The support seem nice.  They only contact you through email so often takes 1-2 weeks to solve issues.  At one point they did call me because my work schedule just did not allow for an accurate solution via email.  The problem is the product has this major flaw when it comes to the quality of its ports for charging.  Wish I could get a recommendation for a camera that has better built materials.  So had to drop a star for this.

This was a waste om money, IMO.  The manual consists of six pictographs and a couple of sentences, the only one relevant to actually using the sight being "Keep the dot on the subject.".  I found a video on-line for the new Olympus camera with a built-in dot sight.  What they expect you to do is hold the camera well away from your body and use the screen on the back of the camera.  The dot is supposed to be used as a general guide, not a precision alignment device.  I regularly shoot wildlife and animals using a 300 mm lens.  The last thing I want to do it hold the camera at arms' length.  That is how you get shaking and other problems.  It would have been far preferable to have had a second mark or guide.  Adjust the dot so that the camera lens, subject and sight are are aligned, and then you can hold the camera in close and use just the dot sight to follow the action.aligned  If you use dot sights like this and have no qualms about holding your camera a couple o feet from your body, it might be worth something to you.  Otherwise, my siggestion is to not buy this sight,

When I first purchased my 3 TB MBL some time ago I was quite pleased.  Network access, remote access, even an android app.  In short, everything my DLink had plus the app and a nicer look on the shelf.  I used it extensively for pulling up photos on my cell phone when I was bored as well as to store my most important files.  I didn't back them up, a back-up drive is something I kept saying I would add later...  14 months later (note the 12 month warranty had just expired) the drive stopped being accessible over the network.  I spent a week and at least 40 hours of my time going back and forth with tech support by e-mail and trying every option they could offer, and the result was that they deemed the unit broken.  They said that the unit can not be opened because that would break the drive inside, and they said that they can't fix it.  They "kindly" offered me a referral to one of several companies that give them a kick back for data recovery services.  Incidentally, I asked two for pricing by e-mail and they didn't respond.  So I took the unit apart and bought a new unit and took that one apart and swapped the drives.  Neither one worked.  After talking to support again it turns out that WD didn't want these to be upgradable because if they were upgradable you could buy a cheap drive instead of a new unit so they code each board to the drive that will be in it.  TO make matters worse, they encoded the drive in a Linux format that no known version of Linux can translate.  Attaching it to my Linux system I couldn't mount it using any utility.  Several Linux recovery programs couldn't do anything either.  Okay, having told you why I will never buy a WD NAS again, I should now tell you how to fix this if you have the same problem that I do:  DiskInternals makes a program called Linux Reader.  That's right, Linux can't read the drive but a program designed to read Linux drives from within Windows can!  Recovering the data to a drive is time consuming though.  I tried it all at once but that froze after a few days.  Then I tried one major directory at a time and that worked better.  But recovering nearly 3 TB of data has taken a very long time!  I'm about 3 MONTHS into the recovery effort and I have about 400 GB remaining.  After starting on my wife's computer because mine is a laptop and causing a few crashes and a lot of delays I finally had to pull out an older computer and set it up just to run this program.  If anyone has the same problem and can't get this solution to work, I'll do my best to help if you simply reply to this review and give me all the details.  If anyone is considering buying a WD NAS, I want to encourage you to buy a DLink model 323 (I think the 325 is the current version but it is missing some features so I'd get an older one) instead.  I've had that thing running for 5 years without a glitch!

This EPSON RUNSENSE SF-110 GPS WATCH/ACTIVITY MONITOR is a runner's watch, not really an activity monitor.  Yes, it records my steps when I wear it, and the app lets me track my weight and other things, but the watch doesn't monitor my sleep, periods of activity/inactivity, or heart rate.  The watch is obviously intended primarily for tracking and analyzing running workouts (times and lap times).  INSTRUCTIONS AND SOFTWARE.  Unfortunately, the instructions for using the watch are extremely confusing to figure out.  For example, to use the chronograph, you must press a complex sequence of buttons on the watch itself.  Specifically, the button steps are:  (1) press D from the Time screen to display Menu; (2) press C/D to highlight Workout, then press A; (3) press C on the Measurement screen to start measuring; (4) press D while measuring to record a lap; (5) press C again to stop measuring; (6) press D while measuring to display Measure Menu screen; (7) press C/D to highlight Stop Workout, then press A.  Did you get all that?  In several sessions, I have tried to record a walking workout, but I've been unable to successfully start and stop a single one.  Worse, when I connect the watch to my PC, and ask the app to upload my watch data, it always returns the message: "There is no data to upload".  This is true even though the watch has been recording my steps, and I can see my step activity by pressing an entirely different, equally complex sequence of watch buttons.  Regarding the software/app, my first mistake was to try to begin using the watch via the Android cellphone app.  The app works, but the Runsense software display is far more complete and easier to understand when you connect the watch and its cradle to a PC.  Maybe I'm spoiled, because I have a [[ASIN:B00GOGV314 Jawbone UP24]] that tracks my steps, sleep, times of activity and inactivity, weight, and workout times through an easy-to-use cellphone app that uploads data automatically whenever the phone is connected to a wireless or cellphone network.  My specific Jawbone model has been discontinued, but there are newer models that do everything mine does, and also track heart rate.  I'm sure that these newer models are equally easy to use.  GPS MAPPING FEATURE.  The reason I ordered this Epson Runsense watch, when I already owned a reliable activity tracker, was that I wanted the GPS feature that is supposed to map and calculate the routes and distances travelled in workouts.  However, the Runsense SF-110 can hardly ever locate a GPS signal for me, and repeatedly returns the message "Failed" .  The instructions say that the GPS feature requires being somewhere with no overhead obstructions--which I guess means you have to be outdoors.  However, when I go outdoors and press the buttons for "GPS location",  the watch can't find a signal.  So no maps, no distances for me!  I do live in a hilly area, but my cellphone and tablets are always able to find GPS locations easily, even indoors.  TIME KEEPING.  The watch has a large, easy-to-read time display, and it keeps good time.  There is a button that lights up the time display when you press it.  The plastic watchband is attractive and has a gazillion little holes, so it will fit any size wrist without resizing.  BLUETOOTH PAIRING. The watch was easy to pair with my Samsung Galaxy S4 cellphone.  It is possible that my EPSON RUNSENSE SF-110 GPS WATCH/ACTIVITY MONITOR is a defective lemon, given that I can't get the GPS or the data upload features to work.  However, even if those worked, I would still find the button-pushing sequences too complex to be useable on a daily basis.  Consequently, I rate this product at 1 star ("I hate it" on the official Amazon scale).

So it’s 2014 and I finally entered the world of touch screen computing and like a good sucker, I dished out $28 for the Kensington Virtuoso Pro Pen 39526 WW (Stylus and Pen combo).  It arrived before the computer, but I decided to examine it nonetheless to see what makes a stylus a stylus.  First of all, I didn’t like the feel and weight of this item and the pen leaked on my finger.  The pen’s tip is a heavy weight metal, which makes it good for writing, but when it’s flipped for screen use, the heaviness is on the top of the stylus (where the pen is) instead of at the bottom.  I don’t like that type of imbalance for drawing.  I discovered that this item is an overpriced do-hickey that anyone can make at home from practically anything that you have lying around the house.  For the how-to video instructions on how to make your own stylus to suit your personal taste and/or needs you can check out the websites that I’ve listed below and/or read the summary that follows. Our smartphone, computer, or tablet’s touch screen senses touch because our fingers produce electrons, hence sending an electrical message to the screen that allows us to write or draw.  Therefore to make a stylus, we need something that will behave as a conductor between our finger and the screen.  Metal is a conductor.  Inexpensive good metal conductors include aluminum, a metal rod (such as an antenna barrel or metal pen), a paper clip, a naked copper wire, or the metal spring that’s inside an old pen.  A wet sponge or Q-Tip can also conduct electricity and neither of them scratches the screen.  To design a scratch-free stylus, insert a strip of sponge or Q-Tip into a metal casing (such as an old pen or a slender antenna tube that you’ve cut to size).  Each should fit tightly into the barrel in order to make a good contact and connection with your finger and the metal casing.  Wet the tip of that sponge or Q-Tip and use it on your touch screen.  If you don’t have a metal barrel available, you can use a cheap plastic pen.  Drill two holes, one on either side of the plastic barrel.  If you don’t have a small drill handy, you can burn the holes through the pen using a heated ice pick or paper clip.  Insert the sponge (make sure it protrudes through the pen’s tip and that it also makes contact with the holes that you drilled).  The final step is to insert a paper clip or a stripped copper wire through the holes and the sponge that you packed into the pen’s barrel.  Next, wrap the wire tightly around the pen’s barrel.  When you write, make sure your finger(s) touch the wire, which will transmit your electronic touch to the sponge and your tablet’s screen.  For the visual instructions, watch the following You Tube videos: Make a stylus with practically any gadget that you have at home or office: [...] [...]  Design a fancy one from scratch: [...]

This is a reasonably attractive leather case. About a third of the cover on the spine side has a pebbled texture while the rest of the cover is smooth.  The Good: I really like Belkin's four corner mounting system. It's very secure and doesn't interfere at all with using the tablet.  The inner material is a soft micro-fiber that's gentle on your screen.  It doesn't add a lot of weight to an already heavy tablet.  There are a plethora of positions the tablet can be stood in. But...(see below)  A stylus loop is included.  The Not-So Good: If mounted in the case so you can use the rear camera through the camera cut-out all the controls as well as the 3.5mm headphone jack are exposed.  This also means that to charge your tablet you have to leave the cover open or pull your tablet out and mount it backwards to access the charging port and protect the screen at the same time.  The front cover has 20 very shallow grooves pressed into the micro-fiber material of the lining. These catch the bottom edge of the back cover and hold it in place for viewing. These actually hold the tablet in place reasonably well but because of the soft folding 'hinge' design the table is very bouncy at any angle. Good for viewing not so good for typing or playing games.  The case material is thin, 4mm on the front cover and thicker on the back because the stand system. There's also a grooved 'hinge' as part of the front cover which lets the cover flex. This reduces the protection  already marginal case material can provide for the screen.  Because of the Cinema design the front cover can't be folded over flat against the back for comfortable use while off a table.  The elasticized strap that holds the cover closed is flimsy and will certainly stretch out over time.  Cover 'creep' happens very easily on this model. When strapped closed the cover can, because it's 'hinged', slide up exposing the screen.  There's a notch cut into the back cover that allows you to flip the cover around and stand the tablet up at a high angle. This position is very unstable and I would never use it.  I wouldn't recommend this cover. It just doesn't offer enough protection for an expensive tablet.

The Salange 7500 LUX Mini Home Projector is a barebones projector that provides good brightness, good focus, full-sized HDMI input, but not much else.  Connect a FireTV or AndroidTV device to this projector and you have all of the "smart" features that you'll need.  So the good of this projector is that it is somewhat compact, not as small as the pico projectors, but small enough...  it's about the size of a bread box (as the saying goes).  The focus ring is fairly tight, meaning that once you find your focus, it won't move.  The USB outputs enough amps to power a FireTV and overall, it is very straightforward to use.  The bad is that there is no keystone adjustment, not even the analogue kind that tilts the lens.  The resolution is only 720p and I found that projecting at a shorter range of around 9 feet, I'm able to see every pixel on the projected image.  At a longer distance, the pixels may meld together better.  In summary, this is a low-priced projector that will project an image through an HDMI input.  If that's all you're looking for, this will work, but if you're looking for something that you may set up more permanently, this isn't it.

I attached 11 pictures as follows.  1 - picture list of parts 2 - photo of specifications 3 - photo of features 4 - camera's resolution menu 5 - camera's frame_rate menu 6 - camera's  white_balance menu 7 - camera's  scene  menu 8 - camera's  setup  menu 9 - camera's  anti_shake  menu 10 - camera's  exposure  menu 11 - camera's ISO menu

[[VIDEOID:e88b9f67b8b32ec1c6281d91851e23f3]] After using a rented Crane 2 for a while, I decided to get something more robust for my own. I did a LOT of research, and all hands pointed to the DJI Ronin S being the best build quality for the money, and having owned DJI equipment before I knew it had a solid reputation already. It seemed a little more expensive than the ones I wanted to buy, but then I found this "essentials kit", which forgoes some of the bells and whistles and gives you just the stabilizer and battery for a price that made it VERY competitive! So I got this thinking it's the deal of the year, until I had issues the first time using it on a job. One of the motors appears to be faulty and would vibrate like crazy at certain angles. I'm attaching the video I sent to DJI's tech support team to help troubleshoot the issue. Initially I thought I had done something wrong, so I spent some time researching online and tried following some of the balance guides and firmware updates, etc, hoping something would solve the issue. But it remains. Then I found someone in a film making forum mention that he had to go through 3 or 4 Ronin S essential kits before getting one that worked... apparently the first 3 would all spaz out in what sounded similar to this one. I got nervous and reached out to the seller wondering if there was just a bad batch of Ronin S essential kits out there. I was told to contact DJI because they might have a fix for it instead. Gullible me, I reached out to DJI who sort of gave me the run around trying different things again for them, and proving I had done them via photos and videos (they wanted to see a screenshot of what firmware, etc, what kind of vibrations, results of balance tests), and this back and forth went on for a while UNTIL I MISSED MY RETURN WINDOW ON THE UNIT. I shouldn't have listened, I should have just returned it and gotten another one hoping that wouldn't be a lemon, but I wanted to be courteous and try DJI in case the problem was actually me. Thankfully, I'm under DJI's warranty so I can send it in for them to properly repair or replace. But to do that, I need to package it up and ship across the country, which could take up to a week, wait 1-2 weeks for the actual repair, then another week for it get back to me. That means close to another month of not having the stabilizer that never worked properly when I bought it over a month ago! I've seen what it can do, and I'm confident that when I get the stabilizer back it will work better (and I intend to update my rating when that happens to reflect it working),  but my experience with this thing so far has NOT been a positive one! There may be a quality control issue with some of the S essential kits, so be prepared to return them and/or deal with repair if it doesn't work right out of the box.

Let me start by stating that I received this camera as part of the Vine program. I have used it for almost 2 months now exclusively and this review is based on this. I also own a Canon Rebel (which I used for real photographic ventures like special events, concerts and travel) and a Sony Cyber-shot (which I had be using for more 'quickie' picture taking as it is smaller and I can fit it in my pocket).  Although I found the picture quality far superior with the Fujifilm XF1 than the Sony, the complexity of the Fujifilm along with the high price tag, make it seem like it should be comparable to what I paid for the Rebel. To be honest, the picture quality and the flexibility/lenses of the Rebel far outweigh the Fujifilm XF1. The way I look at it, the XF1 is a really nice point and shoot. If you are looking for a quality, sleek, stylish camera which is a step above the other pocket sized cameras (especially when it comes to low-light picture capability), and you have several $100 to spend, then the investment may be worth it for you. I just think that if you are a true photographer and want to take quality pictures in the $500 price range, then go for an entry level DSLR which will give you the flexibility of interchanging lenses. I am rating this as a 4 star because, honestly, it's a great camera. It's the price, and what you get for it, that has me shaving one star off the rating.

I've tried a similar box to this one, I'm familiar with the concept and I really REALLY wanted to like it. But sadly, it needs some more quality control. I've always loved the idea of a Android powered head unit, I've seen aftermarket ones sold for popular car models, and I was hoping this would give me that functionality without having to replace my radio. But sadly, I can't use this. For starters, it takes a long time to boot. That means you are first going to be greeted with your car's normal radio display, connected to my phone over bluetooth, and then once I've already pulled out of my driveway and started down the road, THEN it finally shows up on my display. That is, if it displays at all. It seems to do this thing in which is boots up, checks for display info, then reboots in the proper resolution. The instructions even imply it does this. The problem is, most of the time my carplay radio will freeze up when it does this. Not only will it get stuck and not display anything, but my radio locks up and MY TIME RESETS. It's the strangest thing. After looking at reviews here, it seems at least one other reviewer said it make his car radio go crazy, so I don't really understand what this thing is doing that might cause that, but at least it isn't just me! And even when it DOES work, it has a similar problem to other Android-powered adapters like this: it is a cold boot every time, with no idea what the time/date is. This is a major issue because if I don't supply an internet connection, the mini computer in this reverts to the wrong date/time on the display which is SUPER annoying. Ideally, they should design this to have a small battery to "sleep" like a phone, so it can retain time/date function and wake up faster than a complete boot. The way this is designed right now is really a subpar experience, assuming you can get it to boot up at all on your system.

I liked the camera so much I bought the outdoor one as well.  This camera has motion detection that will follow the motion. It actually made my son nervous and thought someone was controlling it. I explained that it detects motion and follows that which is ideal should someone get inside the house.  The camera does have two way Audio that works well and only has a slight lag but is fairly clear.  I am able to monitor the cameras from the app but this is where the problem starts. The app is adjustable to some degree, the problem that I’ve explained to the manufacturer is they require each camera to have separate cloud storage at a separate fee per month. The cloud storage should be based on the account and total account cloud usage. This helps with better networking as well as retaining cloud activity in one central location. If the motion detection were capturing someone who did eventually make it inside, that sequence from camera to camera would be great if it all linked in one cloud account. The problem is that LongPlus has not done this and you pay separate fees per camera.  Not pleased but it is the only company I’ve seen that has an indoor camera and an outdoor camera (where the outdoor camera is battery powered).

We got rid of satellite TV a couple of years ago. We have two TVs in the house, but only one HD over the air tuner. We're looking to get another tuner, so with a second tuner, we need a second antenna. We tested this antenna with our existing tuner. It requires a USB port to be plugged in. We didn't attach it to our wall since we plan to use it in a different room and this was just a test. My husband held it up and was able to get a clear picture with it. Our living room has a two story ceiling. Our other antenna is up pretty high on the wall. The higher it was, the better the reception. The antenna is comparable to our other one in quality and performance. Now that we have another good antenna, we will go forward with purchasing a new tuner.  *I received this for free in exchange for my honest & unbiased review. My thoughts & opinions are my own.

The product arrived very quickly, within 4 days. When I saw the box at the doorstep and went to pick it up. was shocked at how heavy the relatively small box was. Good sign! At least I knew it wasn't made out of ' Plastic from China'. My previous  Desktop 6 CD changer had long stopped working, as it would not 'read' discs as was the case with the previous one . After reading multiple reviews of multiple 'Table Top" multi-CD changers (all- in  -ones) It seems that is a recurring theme.  I have a few hundred CDs and already started Ripping them to hear on the phone via Bluetooth, but if I could just find a single CD player that would be reliable with Blue Tooth and perhaps even a slot for a USB stick (I could get into that too).  This player seemed to be the ticket and it is. The user guide is very large with pictures,  though the instructions are not 'the best', but the player really is simple enough. If you can work one in a car, then this is no different.  I connected the speakers which didn't take long, the ones from my previous player, and was up and running within 5 minutes. T . The sound quality is far better than my previous player which size wise was much larger.   . Actually, from the time I took it out of the box to the time I was listening to the first CD in it, was about 20 minutes (with the Blue tooth connected by then too). I shared some photos with a CD beside the player in this review so you can compare how big it is. The volume for my 2 bedroom condo is great, wouldn't need anything any louder, and it's clear sound (even with the likely very cheap speakers I have right now). So far, it's only Day 1 having it, but no complaints so far. Nicely, had just received an offer for Amazon Prime the same day, so have been using the blue tooth to take advantage of that. If you need a player that won't skip during an earthquake, this seems to be the one.   I tried playing a CD recorded using Windows Media Player, a variety of song copied down from YouTube put to CD, and the CD worked fine. Was also able to insert a USB stick with Music copied to it from the computer and with the help of the Very Rapid Response Customer Service was enabled to work through a 'glitch' on my end in regard to how the player reads music stored on the USB device (which happens to be , 'a separate folder for each ripped CD' )

This camera was highly recommended from a very trusted source, and the reviews were mostly positive. I expected a lot from it, and for a brief period, (maybe 4 months) it worked well. Then the problems began. Lens errors. Yes, despite Canon's insistence that this sort of thing "never" happens, the mechanical lens error you will read about in other people's reviews (known issue with this camera) of this camera happened to me. I bought it hoping they wouldn't, but alas, I am apparently a sucker. Next time, I shall heed the warnings of my fellow Amazonites. No more Canon products for me....  As time wore on, the lens error became more and more of a problem. After another three months, I called Canon. The (arrogant) tech support guy just said, "Oh, this happens when the batteries are weak."  "Oh really?" I said, unwrapping a new set of Energizer AA's. "Let me just stick these puppies in here and see what happens... Huh. Well, would you look at that. So much for your battery theory pal."  After embarrassing him thusly, his only "solution" was to send the camera in for Canon to look at it (at my expense). This would take a minimum of six weeks, after which there was no guarantee I would not continue to have the same problem with the camera. I mean, come on--a design defect is a design defect, after all. I was certainly not the first person to have this problem with this camera. Not only that, but no camera for SIX WEEKS?? I would not have survived.  Sadly, before I could pack the thing up and send it in, I dropped it from the staggering height of three feet. You guessed it, end of camera. Canon makes cheap crap with poor durability and cheap lens mechanisms.  So guess what I did?  Yup, I bought myself a Nikon. I've had three of them with ZERO problems over the years. This is number four. My replacement is working beautifully, and is smaller to boot. Excellent image quality too. I will not buy a Canon again. Fool me once, it's your fault. Fool me twice, it's mine.  Buyer beware.

Never thought I would want an all-in-one computer because you can't expand it at all, but with USB you don't really need to, as you can add external USB drives or devices (printers, scanners, etc.) easily or even use USB flash drives.  Of course you can't add memory, but this comes with 8 gig, which is really all you need these days, unless you are a gamer.  This has a nice large display screen with a clear picture.  Will be great for watching video.  Has a built in microphone and video camera.  Tried the microphone with Windows 10 assistant Cortana and it works great (even though it warned me that it might not).  Comes with Windows 10 pre-installed.  All you have to do is customize a few local settings (time zone, language, etc.) and you are ready to go.  Because everything is built in to the screen, setup time is very fast.  You plug it in and turn it on.  You do have to put batteries in the wireless mouse and keyboard, but they are included, as was the USB stick needed for the wireless devices.  Was up and running in no time.  The wireless mouse and keyboard is nice, but the keyboard is a flat keyboard and I much prefer an ergo keyboard, so I probably won't use the keyboard.  My biggest problem in the past with wireless devices is that they go through batteries fast.  This keyboard and mouse have on/off switches, so you aren't using up the battery when you aren't using the computer.  With everything built into one unit, you might think it would be pretty heavy.  It is heavy, but most people could move it without difficulty.  There is a lot to like about this system and not much to dislike.  I was disappointed that it didn't have more USB ports.  There were only 2 on the back and 2 on the side.  One of the 4 is used for a tiny USB stick that is used for wireless communication with the keyboard and mouse.  You'll quickly run out of USB ports, so you may need a USB hub, if you have more than a couple of devices to hook up.  The 500 gig hard drive is a little small by today's standards, but should be big enough for most people.  For lots of data storage an external source is really more ideal than saving on the C: drive.  You might be able to get a little more computer for less money, but it seems to me that the pricing is not that bad, considering what you get.  Also includes McAfee internet security and some cloud storage (although small and you have to do things to increase it or pay for more space).  Overall not a bad value and ideal for limited space.  No cords and wires to deal with.  You have only the power cord to deal with.  I was also impressed that it included an optical drive.  Many newer systems (especially laptops) don't include a DVD drives anymore.  Performance seemed a little slow, but nothing significant.  I think most people will be happy with this system and it will meet the needs of all but the most demanding users.

I got this internet radio and tried it for a few weeks. At first I was happy with it - the sound quality is quite good, it seemed pretty easy to set up, and I was able to find my favorite NPR station streams as well as connect to my Pandora stations. But after the first two weeks or so, I started having problems with connectivity.  - I'd come home from work and the thing would be on, not playing, with "error connecting to network" or some similar message flashing on the screen. The best way to solve this seemed to be to turn it off, then turn it back on, and then it would work (for a while).  - While listening, it frequently stops playing in the middle of a song. Then the display says either "Buffering" for a few minutes, or more frequently, will actually say "Connecting" for up to five minutes at a time! This happens, on average, every 4-6 minutes. The thing is, I have a wireless router upstairs on the same floor as the radio (Apple Time Capsule) and the wireless signal is quite strong. The other devices that connect to this router never have a problem with connectivity - devices that include a Roku box, a Chumby, my iPhone, my HP netbook, my Macbook, etc.  The problem with losing connectivity started infrequently but got worse and worse over the past two weeks or so, to the point where I can't really listen to music on it at all anymore. So I'm returning it. Too bad, because in terms of looks, sound, and basic functionality, it had everything I wanted.

I would give this one star, except for the cheap price.  The number one problem with mine is that the heart rate monitor does not accurately measure heart rate, or even close.  After 15-20 minutes of running, the readings fluctuated wildly.  At first it was 120, then 131, then a few seconds later 85!  I tried looser and tighter band settings, and also tried rotating the watch to position the sensor on different areas of my wrist (to detect pulse better), and it still never worked correctly.  I'm not sure if mine is just defective, or this product is poorly designed.  There are also a few other minor issues, such as the dull screen (see photo - very hard to read when outdoors) and also my device has randomly shut off a few times.  Overall, I would not recommend this product for anyone.  It's too bad, though, because the design is nice (as is the price) and the bluetooth connectivity and software work well.

Sanyo DS25390 is a simple, no- frills television that offers enough features to get by but is lacking in some of the basics that we now take for granted in television sets, making it a questionable choice of sets for many consumers. Starting with the remote control, this television is very limited and quite out of date. Besides the usual keys (the ten numbered buttons, power button, etc.), you get a set- up key, CC key (closed captioning), Enter, and a Previous Channel selector. The various settings for the television can be accessed from the remote using the menu button, and they include the usual options (changing brightness, color, etc.). There isn't much more to it than that and the control itself has an old- fashioned look to it.  Picture quality on Sanyo DS25390 is okay, but nothing exceptional. The sharpness is the greatest weakness of this Sanyo television. Depending on your distance from the screen, the images seem a little fuzzy on the edges. At a good distance, this problem is minimized well enough that most people will not notice but it is definitely not as sharp as the picture on most newer models.  Picture quality is most important, but one other important consideration when choosing any television is its features and friendliness to accessories. Sanyo DS25390 has limited abilities and limited features. Chief among these is the limits on input. There are only a few input and output jacks and, because of this, there is no way to directly hook up a DVD player or video camera. This fact alone would probably discourage most people from considering this television. You could probably connect a DVD player or other device by going through a VCR first, but how many among is still own a VCR? I realize this television set was considered acceptable during its day, but it is difficult to overlook things like this.  One other problem I have with this television is the sound quality. There is only one small speaker, so the sound delivered is in mono. This certainly reduces the sound quality when watching certain movies or music video. The sound is acceptable for most television shows because the need for stereo quality isn't important. But it isn't very good for certain films and other programs that are greatly enhanced with stereo sound.  Overall, Sanyo Model DS25390 is a difficult television set to evaluate. It seems adequate in some ways and it was probably considered a good television set when it was first introduced. But compared to modern televisions, I cannot imagine anyone buying this set. The fact that a DVD player cannot be directly hooked up to this television is reason enough to reject it. Add to that the picture quality and its lack of sharpness and you have a television set that is too flawed to recommend. It's inexpensive, yes. But you most certainly get what you pay for.

You might think that with GPS on most cell phones these days you wouldn't need a standalone GPS, but this can still be very handy for navigation and gives you extras you don't get with a cell phone.  If you are making a long trip or driving in unfamiliar places, this is what you want.  The screen is nice and large; bigger than a typical cell phone screen.  If you have an older vehicle that doesn't have a Bluetooth connection to your cell phone, this Tom Tom has a feature to link to your phone so that you can talk on your phone through the Tom Tom; it is easy to set up and works well.  There is also a "find my car" feature, if you forget where you parked.  I haven't tested that yet, but it could come in handy.  This unit also contains walking directions, so that if you are trying to go somewhere on foot in an area you are not familiar with, the unit will guide you.  Some of the older Tom Toms had that feature; some did not.  If you have previously owned a Tom Tom, you will find the interface slightly different, but not complicated to figure out.  If you haven't had a Tom Tom before, you might manage to learn how to use the unit quicker than someone who has owned a previous model.  One thing that always bugged me with Tom Tom (and it is still in this version); you enter addresses backwards; you put in city and state first; then street name and direction and then street number last.  It works from the broadest part of the address down to the narrowest.  There are several pluses of this GPS over just a cell phone application, but I haven't had time to check them all out, such as real time traffic reports.  Also, if you plan to drive at all in Europe, Tom Tom seems to have better maps for Europe than other GPS competitors.  I don't know if this unit comes with world maps or just U.S. maps.  As far as accuracy, the device was off by about 1/2 block on my first destination.  It said that I had reached there, but wasn't quite there yet.  It's close enough so that most of the time you should be fine and maybe some destinations are harder to pinpoint than others.  I don't think you will be disappointed at all with this unit.  There is no power cord adapter, so you have to charge it through USB or in your car with the included adapter.  The older Tom Tom units had power plugs included for various countries (about 6 different ones I think).  With USB everywhere, I guess the need for an A/C adapter is not as important as it once was.  Almost a 5-star unit, but not quite there.

So, I have this backpack, and it is kinda weird. You have to see my pictures as it is a different style than what is pictured in the description. I don't really care for it as it is awkward to handle.  --It is a basic looking back pack. I like the bright orange in contrast to the black. That is likely the only feature I really like. --The bag is deep. It measures roughly 21" long, this includes the top portion being unfolded, as that is usable space if need be. @17" wide --The access to the main compartment is a velcro closure with adjustable hook straps for added security (and to keep added length folded down). Unlike many backpacks, there is just one slit opening across the top with velcro to close. Most backpacks have a zipper closure, as well as going a bit down the sides, for ease of access. I'm not fond of how my bag is styled in that way. --Inside the great nylon cavern of the main compartment, it splits into several more compartments and pockets, including a lightly padded pocket. --exterior pockets included two open side pockets that would be great to stick a water bottle in or an umbrella. --two good sized compartments on the front- one zippered, one velcro flap closure. --one rear zip pocket, as well as what appears to be a pass through strap of material- perhaps for use on a luggage handle? --padded shoulder straps are comfortable. Straps include d-rings for clipping keys, or whatever to. --overall feel is good -I cant for the life of me find the USB port that it claims to have. I don't see anywhere for headphones, etc.. so I don't know what is going on.  The part that makes me really not like it is the main opening. First, it is deep, which can be good or bad depending on your needs. But, the top folds over way to easily. So it takes some major rooting around to find stuff, and the flap wants to fold down, and if it manages to fold down, it is easily closed tight by the velcro. This is super annoying if you want to get into and out of the bag repeatedly. I'm just like STAY OPEN. If it zipped, it would be better, then at least if it folder over it would be easy enough to get it, but with the velcro closing it up, it is irritating.  The other aspect that I dont care for is the hooks on the front. Well, they are anchored on the front, but since they are used to help keep bag closed (in addition to the velcro), when they aren't hooked, they are dangling down the front. So you have those kicking around in the front, and the shoulder straps and all its extras kicking around in the back. It isn't as clean or stream lined as it could be. It feels frumpy, I am sorry to say. I was hoping to replace my every day carry bag- I like to have a little cross body purse, plus a back pack for all my additional daily needs. I bought a Targus PA248U Notebook Chill Pad to cool down my laptop, in an effort to extend the life of the system. Almost from the beginning it was problematic and represents one of the worst purchases I have ever made when it comes to computer equipment.  When it was brand new it worked pretty well, but it did not last. After a couple of weeks it started to get louder and louder and finally one of the fans decided to quit working. I disassembled the Chill Pad and oiled the fan’s spindle, and that helped it for a couple of weeks before it did it again. So, to keep the thing working I had to take it apart every couple of weeks and oil the fans. This was a nuisance but was not really a big deal as I have been through this kind of effort with other fans in the past.  Then it began to just cut off and on intermittently. I traced it back to a short in the switch. By keeping some tension on the cord I was able to keep it running.  Finally, this evening it was making loud noises again and all at once it made a loud pop and cut off completely. It had self-destructed - the fans finally developed some wobble and the blades hit the enclosure, snapping some of the blades. So, I don’t believe I even got 6 months of total runtime out of this thing and it’s in the trash.  Targus usually makes good stuff, but this particular Chill Pad is absolute junk. I cannot recommend this solution under any circumstances.

I bought this because my laptop keyboard began to fail.  Pros - Inexpensive - I paid $19, including tax - Both the keyboard and mouse work with Windows with no problems - so far. - Batteries were included - 1 AA for the mouse and 2 AAA for the keyboard. - Includes all standard/function keys plus dedicated media buttons.  Shortcut keys for email, web home page, power and calculator. - All of the shortcut keys work with no config - except the email key - see cons below.  On the con side: - There is no LED to indicate that the keyboard is on.  If there is supposed to be, the LED on my keyboard  doesn't work. - If you use Gmail or another online service, the email shortcut key may not be usable.  I use Gmail and when I pressed the email key, Windows Mail launched. If this can be configured to work with online email services, Logitech provides no instructions in the box. - Unifying receiver is incompatible with newer Logitech software and devices. - The mouse is bare bones - left and right click buttons and a scroll wheel with middle click function.  No next/back buttons, no tilt scroll wheel.  This mouse is best for emergencies or for temporary use until you get a new mouse with better features, etc.  Bottom line - you get what you pay for.
